our_argument_summary: Our review says 11-12 Hz is an 'attenuation valley' in MT surveys — a zone of low signal quality between the Schumann fundamental at ~8 Hz and first harmonic at ~14 Hz — and that working geophysicists deliberately avoid this frequency range. We cite DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4 labeled as a 'Magnetotelluric' source.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-009 has NO independent validation logic in monitor.py. The only telluric domain is Domain 25, which validates WIN-008's 11.7 Hz cutoff with predicted=11.7, observed=11.7, error=0.0%. WIN-009 appears only in inject_ai_layer.py's confirmed_wins registry as a static entry with title 'Telluric ~12 Hz literature peak', data_source='Various', status='confirmed'. There is no calculation, no data fetch, no comparison — it is declared confirmed by fiat.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded. Both the claim and its 'confirmed' status are static strings. No script in the repository computes or validates a 12 Hz peak from any dataset.
data_sources_used: None fetched for WIN-009. Domain 25 (WIN-008) cites 'IRIS / Geometrics MT data' but the observed value 11.7 Hz is also hardcoded with 0.0% error.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None needed — the WIN is never actually tested. It is a static declaration.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not currently mention that WIN-009 has no validation logic in the dome's code. This is a missed opportunity. The review treats WIN-009 as a scientific claim to be rebutted, but the dome's own codebase doesn't even attempt to validate it — it's asserted without computation.

holes_found.description: DOI citation may be incorrect. Our review links to DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4 with anchor text 'Magnetotelluric'. This DOI does not resolve to a known magnetotelluric textbook. The closest ISBN match (978-3-540-71031) points to 'Mathematical Methods in Electro-Magneto-Elasticity' by Parton & Kudryavtsev — an entirely different field. The standard Berdichevsky & Dmitriev MT textbook has DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-77814-1. If a dome defender clicks our source link and finds it leads to an unrelated book, it undermines credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the DOI resolves to the intended magnetotelluric reference. If incorrect, replace with the correct DOI (likely 10.1007/978-3-540-77814-1 for Berdichevsky & Dmitriev 2008, or a specific MT survey paper that documents the spectral minimum between Schumann modes).

holes_found.description: The term 'attenuation valley' is imprecise. 'Attenuation' implies active signal reduction by some medium or process. The 11-12 Hz region has lower natural EM power because it falls between Schumann resonance modes — this is a spectral minimum in the SOURCE spectrum, not attenuation. A dome defender could argue we're mischaracterizing the physics. The correct term would be 'spectral minimum' or 'inter-modal trough'.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider replacing 'attenuation valley' with 'spectral minimum between Schumann modes' or 'inter-modal trough' for greater precision. The underlying point is sound — less natural signal power exists at 12 Hz than at the 7.83 or 14.3 Hz peaks.

holes_found.description: The claim that 'working geophysicists deliberately avoid this frequency range in MT inversions' conflates two different phenomena. The well-documented frequency range that MT practitioners specifically avoid is the AMT dead band at 1-5 kHz (the transition between lightning-sourced and controlled-source signals). At 11-12 Hz, signal-to-noise IS lower than at the Schumann peaks, but geophysicists don't specifically 'avoid' this range — they simply note reduced natural signal power there. Overstating the avoidance makes our argument vulnerable to a technically knowledgeable challenge.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to something like 'this range has reduced natural signal power between the Schumann fundamental and first overtone, resulting in lower signal-to-noise ratios in MT surveys' rather than claiming geophysicists 'deliberately avoid' it.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own code doesn't validate WIN-009. While monitor.py has Domain 25 for WIN-008 (11.7 Hz cutoff), WIN-009 appears only as a static 'confirmed' entry in inject_ai_layer.py with no calculation, no data fetch, and no error metric. This makes WIN-009 a pure assertion rather than even a computed claim. Our review could note this alongside the scientific rebuttal.

A more precise spectral argument: Schumann resonances have ~20% spectral width. The first mode at 7.83 Hz has a half-power bandwidth extending to roughly 9.4 Hz; the second mode at 14.3 Hz extends down to roughly 11.4 Hz. So 12 Hz sits on the rising LOW-frequency wing of the second Schumann mode — it receives some power from the n=2 mode's tail but is NOT a peak. It's on a slope TOWARD the 14.3 Hz peak. Calling it a 'peak' inverts the actual spectral structure. This is more devastating than simply calling it a valley, because we can locate 12 Hz precisely on the known Schumann spectrum.

The dome's original claim says 'Telluric ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets' but names no specific datasets. The dome model site says 'Multiple independent MT literature sources' without citations. If there were published MT spectra showing a prominent 12 Hz peak, the dome could cite them. The absence of specific citations for a claim labeled 'literature peak' is itself telling.
sources: Dome model raw text: WIN-009 entry

kernel_of_truth: There IS genuine complexity in the ELF spectrum around 10-14 Hz. Local conditions (cultural noise from power systems, geological resonances, near-field coupling from infrastructure) can produce enhanced power in this range that might appear as a 'peak' in individual MT survey datasets. Additionally, the second Schumann mode's low-frequency wing does contribute some power at 12 Hz. So someone examining a specific MT dataset COULD observe elevated power near 12 Hz under particular conditions. However, this would be a local/survey-specific feature, not a universal 'telluric peak' — and it would be better explained by Schumann resonance physics and cultural noise than by disc cavity resonance.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-009 claims that the telluric frequency spectrum shows a 'prominent ~12 Hz peak' consistent across independent datasets. Our review categorizes this as 'Refuted by Data' and argues that 11-12 Hz is an attenuation valley rather than a peak. The verdict direction is correct, but three issues reduce the argument's robustness.

First, and most concerning, our cited source may be wrong. The hyperlink points to DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-71031-4 with anchor text 'Magnetotelluric,' but this DOI does not appear to resolve to a recognized magnetotelluric textbook. The closest ISBN match leads to a Springer volume on electro-magneto-elasticity — a completely unrelated field. The major Berdichevsky & Dmitriev MT textbook ('Models and Methods of Magnetotellurics,' 2008) has DOI 10.1007/978-3-540-77814-1. If a dome defender follows our link and finds it leads to an irrelevant book, it damages the credibility of an otherwise sound argument. This needs verification and likely correction.

Second, our terminology overstates the case. We call 11-12 Hz an 'attenuation valley' and say geophysicists 'deliberately avoid this frequency range.' The well-known MT dead band is at 1-5 kHz (the transition between lightning-sourced signals and controlled-source AMT), not at 11-12 Hz. The 11-12 Hz range does have lower natural EM power than the Schumann peaks — it falls between the n=1 mode at 7.83 Hz and the n=2 mode at 14.3 Hz — but this is a spectral minimum in the source function, not 'attenuation' in the propagation sense. More precisely, with the Schumann modes having ~20% spectral width, 12 Hz sits on the low-frequency wing of the second mode — receiving diminishing power as you move away from the 14.3 Hz peak. It is demonstrably NOT a peak in the natural EM spectrum. The physics supports our verdict, but the language conflates two different phenomena and could be tightened.

Third, and this strengthens our position considerably, the dome's own codebase provides no validation for WIN-009. The monitor.py audit engine has 39 domains, and the only telluric domain (Domain 25) validates WIN-008's 11.7 Hz cutoff — not WIN-009's 12 Hz peak. In that domain, both 'predicted' and 'observed' are hardcoded to 11.7 Hz with 0.0% error, making it a tautological pass. WIN-009 appears only in inject_ai_layer.py's confirmed_wins array as a static JSON entry with data_source='Various' and status='confirmed.' No script computes a 12 Hz peak from any data source. The dome effectively declares this WIN confirmed without ever testing it. This is a stronger argument than our current approach of solely rebutting the scientific claim — it shows the dome can't even point to its own code producing this result.

The kernel of truth is that the ELF spectrum at 10-14 Hz does have real structure. Individual MT surveys can show varied spectral features in this range depending on local conditions, cultural noise, and time of day. Someone examining a particular dataset could see elevated power near 12 Hz. But the dome's claim of a universal 'prominent ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets' is not supported by the published Schumann resonance spectrum, which has its trough (not peak) in this range. The verdict of 'Refuted by Data' holds, but the supporting text should fix the DOI, tighten the terminology, and add the code-based argument for maximum robustness.
